---
title: "Hardcoded Version vs Dependency Management"
tagline: "What to Do When One Hardcoded Version Overrides Everything Else"
type: scenario
tool: maven
---

#### ! Scenario What to Do When One Hardcoded Version Overrides Everything Else:

This scenario shows what happens when you have a conflict between a version defined in a BOM (Dependency Management) and a version explicitly declared in your dependencies.

#### ! Example

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 4.4
graph LR
    Root[Project Root] -- imports --> BOM[demo-bom:1.0.0]
    Root --> LibA[lib-a:2.0.0]
    Root --> LibB[lib-b]
    BOM -. suggests .-> LibA_BOM[lib-a:1.0.0]
    BOM -. suggests .-> LibB_BOM[lib-b:1.0.0]
```


```{.xml include="pom.xml" snippet="s4-deps"}
```

#### ! Expect
Explicitly declared versions win over managed suggestions.

#### ! Why
The `<dependencies>` section is the final authority for the project; `<dependencyManagement>` acts only as a default. This priority is why the same graph behaves differently across tools.

#### ! Fix
They might try to change the BOM version, which affects everything. Or they use exclusions.

#### ! Control
Be careful when overriding BOMs. You might break compatibility with other libraries managed by that BOM. If you must override, ensure you test thoroughly.

#### ! Check

 `mvn dependency:tree`

**What to look for:** The explicitly declared version in the tree, rather than the BOM or platform recommendation.

**Conclusion:** A hardcoded version in `<dependencies>` always overrides `<dependencyManagement>`.

#### ! Scales

Overriding BOMs is common when patching a vulnerability before the BOM maintainers release an update.

#### ! Takeaway
Explicit declarations generally trump managed recommendations, allowing for targeted overrides.

#### ! Related
*   [S6: Forcing Transitive Versions via Dependency Management](../scenario-6-forcing/guide.qmd) - How to force a version even against explicit declarations.


#### ! Wild

A common pattern with the [Spring Boot BOM](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/appendix/dependency-versions/index.html) is needing a patched library version before the next Boot release bundles it. For example, when Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228) hit, teams couldn't wait for a coordinated Spring Boot update -- they declared the patched `log4j-core` version directly in `<dependencies>`, overriding the BOM's managed version. Spring's own [documentation](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/how-to/build.html#howto.build.customize-dependency-versions) describes this as the intended escape hatch: a direct `<dependency>` declaration always takes precedence over `<dependencyManagement>`.
